content.text:
<user_query>
In GCP, can I rename a billing account?
</user_query>

Checking current GCP documentation on renaming billing accounts.

[REDACTED]

input.search_term: Google Cloud Platform rename billing account
input.explanation: Confirm current GCP behavior and official guidance for renaming billing accounts.

content.text:
Yes. In GCP you can change the **Cloud Billing account name** — the **display name** shown in selectors, lists, and reports.

**How (console):** Open [Account management in the Cloud Billing console](https://console.cloud.google.com/billing/manage), pick the billing account, then use **Rename billing account** at the top, enter the new name, and save. You may need to refresh to see it everywhere.

**Permissions:** You need `billing.accounts.update`, which the predefined **Billing Account Administrator** role includes. If the button is missing, your user likely lacks that permission on that billing account.

**What this is not:** This renames the **friendly name**, not the underlying billing account **resource ID** (the `01xxxx-xxxxx-xxxxx` style identifier used in APIs and URLs). For naming in the UI and reports, the display name is what you edit.

Official steps: [Modify your Cloud Billing account — Rename a Cloud Billing account](https://cloud.google.com/billing/docs/how-to/modify-billing-account#rename_a_cloud_billing_account).

[REDACTED]

input.explanation: Confirm current GCP project deletion/shutdown behavior and official terminology.
input.search_term: Google Cloud delete project shut down remove

content.text:
Yes. In Google Cloud you **remove** a project by **shutting it down** (deleting it). That stops billing for that project, tears down running resources, and makes the project unusable.

**Console:** Pick the project → **IAM & Admin** → **Settings** (or use the project picker’s project settings) → **Shut down** / delete flow → confirm with the **project ID**.

**CLI:** `gcloud projects delete PROJECT_ID`

**What to expect**

- There is typically a **grace period** (often about **30 days**) during which an owner may **restore** the project before it is gone for good.
- Deletion can be **blocked** until you fix certain setups (for example **liens**, some **Shared VPC** attachments, or other constraints). See [Troubleshooting project deletion](https://cloud.google.com/resource-manager/docs/troubleshooting-project-deletion).

**Permissions:** You need rights to delete the project (commonly **Owner** on the project, or a role that includes `resourcemanager.projects.delete`).

**Note:** The **project ID** is reserved for a while after shutdown; you generally **cannot reuse** the same ID immediately on a brand-new project.

More detail: [Creating, shutting down, and restoring projects](https://support.google.com/googleapi/answer/6251787) and [`gcloud projects delete`](https://cloud.google.com/sdk/gcloud/reference/projects/delete).

[REDACTED]

<user_query>
How do I see what the project does? I don't remember creating it. It's call 'My First Project'. 

Could it be related to a Google Workflow automation?
</user_query>

Here is a practical way to think about it and how to inspect the project.

## Why it is named “My First Project”

That name is **Google’s default** for a new Cloud project. It often appears when something first turns on Google Cloud for your account—for example opening the [Google Cloud Console](https://console.cloud.google.com/), using **Firebase**, enabling an API for **Maps**, **Android Studio / Google Play** tooling, a **Workspace** admin flow, or another product that provisions a Cloud project in the background. So **not remembering creating it is normal**; something may have created it for you with that title.

## How to see what the project “does”

There is no single “purpose” field. You infer it from **what is enabled and what ran**.

1. **Pick that project** in the project selector at the top of Cloud Console.

2. **APIs & Services → Enabled APIs & services**  
   Shows which products are turned on (e.g. Cloud Run, Workflows, BigQuery, Maps, Firebase). Empty or only “Cloud Resource Manager” suggests little intentional use.

3. **IAM & Admin → IAM**  
   See **who has access** (users, groups, service accounts). Unfamiliar service accounts can hint at which product created links (still not always obvious).

4. **Billing**  
   **Billing → Reports** (or link the project to billing and check usage). **No/low spend** usually means idle or barely used.

5. **Compute / common resource UIs** (quick spot checks)  
   **Cloud Run**, **Cloud Functions**, **App Engine**, **Kubernetes Engine**, **Compute Engine → VM instances**, **Storage → Buckets**, **Firestore** (if Firebase). If everything is empty, the project may be unused.

6. **Logs (if anything ever ran)**  
   **Logging → Logs Explorer**, query for recent activity. Quiet logs + no resources + few APIs often means a **shell / default** project.

7. **Organization / folder**  
   If it sits under a company **organization**, an admin or another system may have created it; if it is only under “No organization,” it is often a personal default.

## Could it be related to “Google Workflow automation”?

It depends what you mean:

- **Google Cloud Workflows** (GCP orchestration): **Yes, that runs inside a Cloud project**, and you would see **“Workflows API”** enabled and definitions under **Workflows** in the console. If those are empty and the API is not enabled, it is probably **not** that.

- **Google Workspace / “workflow” style automations** (e.g. things tied to Forms, Sheets, Drive, **Apps Script**): Those can **use** a Cloud project for OAuth / advanced services, but many automations **never** need a dedicated GCP project, or use a **default** one. A generically named “My First Project” **could** be linked if someone (or a wizard) connected Apps Script or an add-on to Cloud—checking **Enabled APIs** and **Apps Script** (script.google.com) project settings is the way to tell.

- **Other “automation”** (Firebase, Zapier-style tools, CI): Same idea—look at **enabled APIs**, **IAM**, and **billing usage**.

**Bottom line:** “My First Project” is **usually a default / idle** project unless you see specific APIs, resources, or spend. Inspecting **Enabled APIs** and whether **Workflows** (or Firebase, Maps, etc.) is enabled is the fastest way to see if it matches your automation theory.

If you tell me whether you mean **GCP Workflows** or **Workspace / Apps Script** style automation, the checklist can be narrowed to two or three exact console pages.

[REDACTED]

<user_query>
I don't see any APIs & Services for "My First Project". However, I don't see it for a new project that has an active API.
</user_query>

Checking how the Cloud Console surfaces “APIs & Services” today — the menu path can vary by UI and permissions.

[REDACTED]

input.explanation: Confirm current Cloud Console paths and common reasons APIs & Services menu is missing.
input.search_term: Google Cloud Console APIs Services menu not showing permissions

content.text:
Here is a concise way to sort this out, because “I don’t see APIs & Services” can mean two different things.

## 1. You cannot find the **menu / page** (navigation)

The label is easy to miss in the newer console. Try these in order.

**A. Use the top search bar** (click the search field at the top of [Google Cloud Console](https://console.cloud.google.com/)) and type:

- `Enabled APIs`
- or `APIs & Services`

Open the result that goes to the **Enabled APIs** (or **API Library**) page.

**B. Open it by URL** (replace `YOUR_PROJECT_ID` with the **project ID**, not the display name):

- Enabled APIs dashboard:  
  `https://console.cloud.google.com/apis/dashboard?project=YOUR_PROJECT_ID`
- API Library (to enable more):  
  `https://console.cloud.google.com/apis/library?project=YOUR_PROJECT_ID`

**C. Confirm the project**  
The project picker at the top must be **exactly** the project you care about. The URL should contain `?project=...` matching that project’s **ID** (often different from the human-readable name).

**D. Account**  
Make sure you are logged into the **same Google account** that owns or was invited to that project.

**E. Permissions**  
If the page opens but says you lack permission, your role may not include listing services (common on org/team projects). Someone with **Owner** / **Editor** / **Browser** (or a custom role with Service Usage read) may need to grant you access. As a check, an owner on the project should always see this for that project.

---

## 2. You **find** APIs & Services, but the **list looks empty**

That is normal for many **“My First Project”** instances: nothing was ever enabled, so the enabled list is empty or nearly empty.

For a **new** project where you *know* an API is “active”:

- The API might be enabled under a **different** project ID than the one you have selected (easy mistake if names are similar).
- Or the “active” API is tied to another surface (e.g. **Firebase** has its own console; it still uses a GCP project, but you might have enabled things there while looking at a different project in the main console).

Again, the **dashboard URL with `?project=`** is the quickest way to remove ambiguity.

---

## 3. CLI cross-check (bypasses the menu entirely)

If `gcloud` is installed and configured:

```bash
gcloud config set project YOUR_PROJECT_ID
gcloud services list --enabled
```

If that lists APIs while the console looked empty, it is almost certainly **wrong project selected in the UI** or a **permissions / account** mismatch. If both are empty, nothing is enabled (or your identity cannot list services).

---

**Summary:** Use **search** for “Enabled APIs” or the **direct dashboard URL** with the correct **`project=`** ID. If you still get nothing on a project you believe uses an API, compare **`gcloud services list --enabled`** for both project IDs side by side—that usually exposes the mismatch immediately.

If you can share (redacted) whether the issue is “can’t find the menu” vs “page loads but list is empty,” and whether both projects are under the same Google account, the next step is narrow and specific.

[REDACTED]

<user_query>
Yes, My First Project has just the basic APIs for monitoring, and nothing has hit it.

It is not related to an automated workflow that I created in Google Workspace stuidio, right?
</user_query>

You can treat it as **almost certainly not related** to automations you built in **Google Workspace Studio** (or similar Workspace-side workflow tools).

**Why:**

1. **Workspace automations run in Google’s Workspace / automation layer.** They do not need your personal **Google Cloud** project named “My First Project” unless you (or an admin) **explicitly** connected advanced features that require a **Google Cloud project** (for example some **Apps Script** “Google Cloud Platform” project linking, or certain enterprise integrations). That is an explicit setup step, not something those tools silently do to a random default GCP project.

2. **What you described is the classic idle default project:** only the usual **monitoring / platform** style APIs and **no usage** means nothing meaningful is calling that project. A workflow that was actually using that project for API calls, triggers, or connectors would normally show **additional enabled APIs** and/or **activity** (logs, metrics, billing) over time.

3. **If it were tied to Workspace automation**, you would usually see a deliberate link somewhere in the product (script properties, connector settings, “Cloud project” / OAuth / advanced services), not just a dormant “My First Project.”

**Caveat (rare):** If someone **manually** pointed a script or integration at that project’s ID, it *could* be related—but that would be intentional and would tend to leave traces (extra APIs, executions, or at least IAM/service accounts you would recognize). With **no traffic** and **only baseline APIs**, that scenario is very unlikely.

So: **your Workspace Studio workflows are not “running on” or “owned by” that idle GCP project in any meaningful way.** “My First Project” is most likely just the default shell Google created when Cloud was first touched for your account.

[REDACTED]

<user_query>
Ok. How do I remove "My First Project" in GCP step by step
</user_query>

input.explanation: Verify current Cloud Console steps to shut down/delete a GCP project.
input.search_term: Google Cloud shut down project IAM Admin Settings 2024 2025

content.text:
Here is a straightforward way to **remove** “My First Project” in GCP. In Google’s terms you **shut down** (delete) the project; it is not usable after that and is **fully removed after the recovery window** (typically about **30 days**, during which an owner can restore it).

### Before you start

1. Open [Google Cloud Console](https://console.cloud.google.com/) and sign in with the account that **owns** the project.
2. In the **project picker** at the top, select **My First Project**.
3. Note the **Project ID** (not the display name “My First Project”). The shutdown screen will ask for this **exact ID**.

### Shut it down in the console (step by step)

1. With **My First Project** selected, open **IAM & Admin** in the left navigation.  
   - If the menu is collapsed, use the **☰** menu, or the top **search** and type `IAM & Admin Settings`.

2. Open **Settings** under **IAM & Admin** (the page is often labeled **Project settings** / IAM & Admin settings).

3. Find **Shut down** (or **Delete project** / similar wording—Google occasionally tweaks labels).

4. Click **Shut down**.

5. When prompted, type the **Project ID** exactly as shown (this confirms you are deleting the right project).

6. Confirm with **Shut down anyway** (or the final confirm button).

If **Shut down** does not appear, your account likely does not have **`resourcemanager.projects.delete`** on that project (for example you are not **Owner**). Someone with **Owner** on the project must do this step or grant you a role that includes project delete.

### After shutdown

- The project **stops working immediately**; **billing is unlinked** and is **not** automatically restored if you undelete later.
- For roughly **30 days** it may still show as “pending deletion”; you can **restore** it from the [Manage resources](https://console.cloud.google.com/cloud-resource-manager) flow if you change your mind.
- The **project ID** stays reserved for a while; you generally **cannot** reuse it right away on a new project.

### Optional: command line

If you use the Google Cloud CLI and have permission:

```bash
gcloud projects delete PROJECT_ID
```

Official reference: [Creating and managing projects — Shut down a project](https://cloud.google.com/resource-manager/docs/creating-managing-projects#shutting_down_projects) (includes the direct **IAM & Admin Settings** link). If deletion fails, see [Troubleshooting project deletion](https://cloud.google.com/resource-manager/docs/troubleshooting-project-deletion) (e.g. **liens** or **Shared VPC** blocking shutdown).

[REDACTED]
